NKJV.1: Now it happened, as He went into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees to eat bread on the Sabbath, that they watched Him closely.
NKJV.2: And behold, there was a certain man before Him who had dropsy.
NKJV.3: And Jesus, answering, spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?”
NKJV.4: But they kept silent. And He took him and healed him, and let him go.
NKJV.5: Then He answered them, saying, “Which of you, having a donkey or an ox that has fallen into a pit, will not immediately pull him out on the Sabbath day?”
NKJV.6: And they could not answer Him regarding these things.
NKJV.7: So He told a parable to those who were invited, when He noted how they chose the best places, saying to them:
NKJV.8: “When you are invited by anyone to a wedding feast, do not sit down in the best place, lest one more honorable than you be invited by him;
NKJV.9: and he who invited you and him come and say to you, ‘Give place to this man,’ and then you begin with shame to take the lowest place.
NKJV.10: But when you are invited, go and sit down in the lowest place, so that when he who invited you comes he may say to you, ‘Friend, go up higher.’ Then you will have glory in the presence of those who sit at the table with you.
NKJV.11: For whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.”
NKJV.12: Then He also said to him who invited Him, “When you give a dinner or a supper, do not ask your friends, your brothers, your relatives, nor rich neighbors, lest they also invite you back, and you be repaid.
NKJV.13: But when you give a feast, invite the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind.
NKJV.14: And you will be  blessed, because they cannot repay you; for you shall be repaid at the resurrection of the just.”
NKJV.15: Now when one of those who sat at the table with Him heard these things, he said to Him, “Blessed is he who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God!”
NKJV.16: Then He said to him, “A certain man gave a great supper and invited many,
NKJV.17: and  sent his servant at supper time to say to those who were invited, ‘Come, for all things are now ready.’
NKJV.18: But they all with one accord began to make excuses. The first said to him, ‘I have bought a piece of ground, and I must go and see it. I ask you to have me excused.’
NKJV.19: And another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to test them. I ask you to have me excused.’
NKJV.20: Still another said, ‘I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.’
NKJV.21: So that servant came and reported these things to his master. Then the master of the house, being angry, said to his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in here the poor and the maimed and the lame and the blind.’
NKJV.22: And the servant said, ‘Master, it is done as you commanded, and still there is room.’
NKJV.23: Then the master said to the servant, ‘Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.
NKJV.24: For I say to you  that none of those men who were invited shall taste my supper.’ ”
NKJV.25: Now great multitudes went with Him. And He turned and said to them,
NKJV.26: “If anyone comes to Me  and does not hate his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters,  yes, and his own life also, he cannot be My disciple.
NKJV.27: And  whoever does not bear his cross and come after Me cannot be My disciple.
NKJV.28: For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not sit down first and count the cost, whether he has enough to finish it—
NKJV.29: lest, after he has laid the foundation, and is not able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him,
NKJV.30: saying, ‘This man began to build and was not able to finish’?
NKJV.31: Or what king, going to make war against another king, does not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?
NKJV.32: Or else, while the other is still a great way off, he sends a delegation and asks conditions of peace.
NKJV.33: So likewise, whoever of you  does not forsake all that he has cannot be My disciple.
NKJV.34: “Salt is good; but if the salt has lost its flavor, how shall it be seasoned?
NKJV.35: It is neither fit for the land nor for the dunghill, but men throw it out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear!”

ESV.1: One Sabbath, when he went to dine at the house of a ruler of the Pharisees, they were watching him carefully.
ESV.2: And behold, there was a man before him who had dropsy.
ESV.3: And Jesus responded to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying,  “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?”
ESV.4: But they remained silent. Then he took him and healed him and sent him away.
ESV.5: And he said to them,  “Which of you, having a son  or an ox that has fallen into a well on a Sabbath day, will not immediately pull him out?”
ESV.6: And they could not reply to these things.
ESV.7: Now he told a parable to those who were invited, when he noticed how they chose the places of honor, saying to them,
ESV.8: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not sit down in a place of honor, lest someone more distinguished than you be invited by him,
ESV.9: and he who invited you both will come and say to you, ‘Give your place to this person,’ and then you will begin with shame to take the lowest place.
ESV.10: But when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when your host comes he may say to you, ‘Friend, move up higher.’ Then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at table with you.
ESV.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.”
ESV.12: He said also to the man who had invited him, “When you give a dinner or a banquet, do not invite your friends or your brothers  or your relatives or rich neighbors, lest they also invite you in return and you be repaid.
ESV.13: But when you give a feast, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind,
ESV.14: and you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you. For you will be repaid at the resurrection of the just.”
ESV.15: When one of those who reclined at table with him heard these things, he said to him, “Blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!”
ESV.16: But he said to him,  “A man once gave a great banquet and invited many.
ESV.17: And at the time for the banquet he sent his servant  to say to those who had been invited, ‘Come, for everything is now ready.’
ESV.18: But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said to him, ‘I have bought a field, and I must go out and see it. Please have me excused.’
ESV.19: And another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to examine them. Please have me excused.’
ESV.20: And another said, ‘I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.’
ESV.21: So the servant came and reported these things to his master. Then the master of the house became angry and said to his servant, ‘Go out quickly to the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame.’
ESV.22: And the servant said, ‘Sir, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.’
ESV.23: And the master said to the servant, ‘Go out to the highways and hedges and compel people to come in, that my house may be filled.
ESV.24: For I tell you,  none of those men who were invited shall taste my banquet.’”
ESV.25: Now great crowds accompanied him, and he turned and said to them,
ESV.26: “If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, yes, and even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.
ESV.27: Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple.
ESV.28: For which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost, whether he has enough to complete it?
ESV.29: Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him,
ESV.30: saying, ‘This man began to build and was not able to finish.’
ESV.31: Or what king, going out to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and deliberate whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?
ESV.32: And if not, while the other is yet a great way off, he sends a delegation and asks for terms of peace.
ESV.33: So therefore, any one of you who does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple.
ESV.34: “Salt is good, but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored?
ESV.35: It is of no use either for the soil or for the manure pile. It is thrown away. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”

NASB.1: It happened that when He went into the house of one of the leaders of the Pharisees on [the] Sabbath to eat bread, they were watching Him closely.
NASB.2: And there in front of Him was a man suffering from dropsy.
NASB.3: And Jesus answered and spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, 'Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?'
NASB.4: But they kept silent. And He took hold of him and healed him, and sent him away.
NASB.5: And He said to them, 'Which one of you will have a son or an ox fall into a well, and will not immediately pull him out on a Sabbath day?'
NASB.6: And they could make no reply to this.
NASB.7: And He [began] speaking a parable to the invited guests when He noticed how they had been picking out the places of honor [at the table], saying to them,
NASB.8: 'When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not take the place of honor, for someone more distinguished than you may have been invited by him,
NASB.9: and he who invited you both will come and say to you, 'Give [your] place to this man,' and then in disgrace you proceed to occupy the last place.
NASB.10: 'But when you are invited, go and recline at the last place, so that when the one who has invited you comes, he may say to you, 'Friend, move up higher'; then you will have honor in the sight of all who are at the table with you.
NASB.11: 'For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.'
NASB.12: And He also went on to say to the one who had invited Him, 'When you give a luncheon or a dinner, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors, otherwise they may also invite you in return and [that] will be your repayment.
NASB.13: 'But when you give a reception, invite [the] poor, [the] crippled, [the] lame, [the] blind,
NASB.14: and you will be blessed, since they do not have [the means] to repay you; for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.'
NASB.15: When one of those who were reclining [at the table] with Him heard this, he said to Him, 'Blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!'
NASB.16: But He said to him, 'A man was giving a big dinner, and he invited many;
NASB.17: and at the dinner hour he sent his slave to say to those who had been invited, 'Come; for everything is ready now.'
NASB.18: 'But they all alike began to make excuses. The first one said to him, 'I have bought a piece of land and I need to go out and look at it; please consider me excused.'
NASB.19: 'Another one said, 'I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to try them out; please consider me excused.'
NASB.20: 'Another one said, 'I have married a wife, and for that reason I cannot come.'
NASB.21: 'And the slave came [back] and reported this to his master. Then the head of the household became angry and said to his slave, 'Go out at once into the streets and lanes of the city and bring in here the poor and crippled and blind and lame.'
NASB.22: 'And the slave said, 'Master, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.'
NASB.23: 'And the master said to the slave, 'Go out into the highways and along the hedges, and compel [them] to come in, so that my house may be filled.
NASB.24: 'For I tell you, none of those men who were invited shall taste of my dinner.''
NASB.25: Now large crowds were going along with Him; and He turned and said to them,
NASB.26: 'If anyone comes to Me, and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, yes, and even his own life, he cannot be My disciple.
NASB.27: 'Whoever does not carry his own cross and come after Me cannot be My disciple.
NASB.28: 'For which one of you, when he wants to build a tower, does not first sit down and calculate the cost to see if he has enough to complete it?
NASB.29: 'Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who observe it begin to ridicule him,
NASB.30: saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish.'
NASB.31: 'Or what king, when he sets out to meet another king in battle, will not first sit down and consider whether he is strong enough with ten thousand [men] to encounter the one coming against him with twenty thousand?
NASB.32: 'Or else, while the other is still far away, he sends a delegation and asks for terms of peace.
NASB.33: 'So then, none of you can be My disciple who does not give up all his own possessions.
NASB.34: 'Therefore, salt is good; but if even salt has become tasteless, with what will it be seasoned?
NASB.35: 'It is useless either for the soil or for the manure pile; it is thrown out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.'

NLT.1: One Sabbath day Jesus went to eat dinner in the home of a leader of the Pharisees, and the people were watching him closely.
NLT.2: There was a man there whose arms and legs were swollen.
NLT.3: Jesus asked the Pharisees and experts in religious law, “Is it permitted in the law to heal people on the Sabbath day, or not?”
NLT.4: When they refused to answer, Jesus touched the sick man and healed him and sent him away.
NLT.5: Then he turned to them and said, “Which of you doesn’t work on the Sabbath? If your son or your cow falls into a pit, don’t you rush to get him out?”
NLT.7: When Jesus noticed that all who had come to the dinner were trying to sit in the seats of honor near the head of the table, he gave them this advice:
NLT.8: “When you are invited to a wedding feast, don’t sit in the seat of honor. What if someone who is more distinguished than you has also been invited?
NLT.9: The host will come and say, ‘Give this person your seat.’ Then you will be embarrassed, and you will have to take whatever seat is left at the foot of the table!
NLT.10: “Instead, take the lowest place at the foot of the table. Then when your host sees you, he will come and say, ‘Friend, we have a better place for you!’ Then you will be honored in front of all the other guests.
NLT.11: For those who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted.”
NLT.12: Then he turned to his host. “When you put on a luncheon or a banquet,” he said, “don’t invite your friends, brothers, relatives, and rich neighbors. For they will invite you back, and that will be your only reward.
NLT.13: Instead, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind.
NLT.14: Then at the resurrection of the righteous, God will reward you for inviting those who could not repay you.”
NLT.15: Hearing this, a man sitting at the table with Jesus exclaimed, “What a blessing it will be to attend a banquet in the Kingdom of God!”
NLT.16: Jesus replied with this story: “A man prepared a great feast and sent out many invitations.
NLT.17: When the banquet was ready, he sent his servant to tell the guests, ‘Come, the banquet is ready.’
NLT.18: But they all began making excuses. One said, ‘I have just bought a field and must inspect it. Please excuse me.’
NLT.19: Another said, ‘I have just bought five pairs of oxen, and I want to try them out. Please excuse me.’
NLT.20: Another said, ‘I just got married, so I can’t come.’
NLT.21: “The servant returned and told his master what they had said. His master was furious and said, ‘Go quickly into the streets and alleys of the town and invite the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.’
NLT.22: After the servant had done this, he reported, ‘There is still room for more.’
NLT.23: So his master said, ‘Go out into the country lanes and behind the hedges and urge anyone you find to come, so that the house will be full.
NLT.24: For none of those I first invited will get even the smallest taste of my banquet.’”
NLT.25: A large crowd was following Jesus. He turned around and said to them,
NLT.26: “If you want to be my disciple, you must, by comparison, hate everyone else — your father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters — yes, even your own life. Otherwise, you cannot be my disciple.
NLT.27: And if you do not carry your own cross and follow me, you cannot be my disciple.
NLT.28: “But don’t begin until you count the cost. For who would begin construction of a building without first calculating the cost to see if there is enough money to finish it?
NLT.29: Otherwise, you might complete only the foundation before running out of money, and then everyone would laugh at you.
NLT.30: They would say, ‘There’s the person who started that building and couldn’t afford to finish it!’
NLT.31: “Or what king would go to war against another king without first sitting down with his counselors to discuss whether his army of 10,000 could defeat the 20,000 soldiers marching against him?
NLT.32: And if he can’t, he will send a delegation to discuss terms of peace while the enemy is still far away.
NLT.33: So you cannot become my disciple without giving up everything you own.
NLT.34: “Salt is good for seasoning. But if it loses its flavor, how do you make it salty again?
NLT.35: Flavorless salt is good neither for the soil nor for the manure pile. It is thrown away. Anyone with ears to hear should listen and understand!”

WEB.1: When he went into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees on a Sabbath to eat bread, they were watching him.
WEB.2: Behold, a certain man who had dropsy was in front of him.
WEB.3: Jesus, answering, spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?”
WEB.4: But they were silent. He took him, and healed him, and let him go.
WEB.5: He answered them, “Which of you, if your son  or an ox fell into a well, wouldn’t immediately pull him out on a Sabbath day?”
WEB.6: They couldn’t answer him regarding these things.
WEB.7: He spoke a parable to those who were invited, when he noticed how they chose the best seats, and said to them,
WEB.8: “When you are invited by anyone to a marriage feast, don’t sit in the best seat, since perhaps someone more honorable than you might be invited by him,
WEB.9: and he who invited both of you would come and tell you, ‘Make room for this person.’ Then you would begin, with shame, to take the lowest place.
WEB.10: But when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when he who invited you comes, he may tell you, ‘Friend, move up higher.’ Then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at the table with you.
WEB.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted.”
WEB.12: He also said to the one who had invited him, “When you make a dinner or a supper, don’t call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor rich neighbors, or perhaps they might also return the favor, and pay you back.
WEB.13: But when you make a feast, ask the poor, the maimed, the lame, or the blind;
WEB.14: and you will be blessed, because they don’t have the resources to repay you. For you will be repaid in the resurrection of the righteous.”
WEB.15: When one of those who sat at the table with him heard these things, he said to him, “Blessed is he who will feast in God’s Kingdom!”
WEB.16: But he said to him, “A certain man made a great supper, and he invited many people.
WEB.17: He sent out his servant at supper time to tell those who were invited, ‘Come, for everything is ready now.’
WEB.18: They all as one began to make excuses.  “The first said to him, ‘I have bought a field, and I must go and see it. Please have me excused.’
WEB.19: “Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I must go try them out. Please have me excused.’
WEB.20: “Another said, ‘I have married a wife, and therefore I can’t come.’
WEB.21: “That servant came, and told his lord these things. Then the master of the house, being angry, said to his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in the poor, maimed, blind, and lame.’
WEB.22: “The servant said, ‘Lord, it is done as you commanded, and there is still room.’
WEB.23: “The lord said to the servant, ‘Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.
WEB.24: For I tell you that none of those men who were invited will taste of my supper.’”
WEB.25: Now great multitudes were going with him. He turned and said to them,
WEB.26: “If anyone comes to me, and doesn’t disregard  his own father, mother, wife, children, brothers, and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he can’t be my disciple.
WEB.27: Whoever doesn’t bear his own cross, and come after me, can’t be my disciple.
WEB.28: For which of you, desiring to build a tower, doesn’t first sit down and count the cost, to see if he has enough to complete it?
WEB.29: Or perhaps, when he has laid a foundation, and is not able to finish, everyone who sees begins to mock him,
WEB.30: saying, ‘This man began to build, and wasn’t able to finish.’
WEB.31: Or what king, as he goes to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?
WEB.32: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sends an envoy, and asks for conditions of peace.
WEB.33: So therefore whoever of you who doesn’t renounce all that he has, he can’t be my disciple.
WEB.34: Salt is good, but if the salt becomes flat and tasteless, with what do you season it?
WEB.35: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile. It is thrown out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”

CSB17.1: One Sabbath, when he went in to eat <sup>[1]</sup> at the house of one of the leading Pharisees, <sup>ⓛ</sup> they were watching him closely. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.2: There in front of him was a man whose body was swollen with fluid.
CSB17.3: In response, Jesus asked the law experts <sup>ⓝ</sup> and the Pharisees, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not?” <sup>ⓐ</sup>
CSB17.4: But they kept silent. He took the man, healed him, and sent him away.
CSB17.5: And to them, he said, “Which of you whose son or ox falls into a well, will not immediately pull him out on the Sabbath day?” <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.6: They could find no answer to these things.
CSB17.7: He told a parable <sup>ⓒ</sup> to those who were invited, when he noticed how they would choose the best places <sup>ⓓ</sup> for themselves:
CSB17.8: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding banquet, don’t recline at the best place, because a more distinguished person <sup>ⓔ</sup>than you may have been invited by your host.
CSB17.9: The one who invited both of you may come and say to you, ‘Give your place to this man,’ and then in humiliation, you will proceed to take the lowest place.
CSB17.10: “But when you are invited, go and recline in the lowest place, so that when the one who invited you comes, he will say to you, ‘Friend, move up higher.’ You will then be honored <sup>ⓕ</sup> in the presence of all the other guests. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who humbles himself will be exalted.” <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.12: He also said to the one who had invited him, “When you give a lunch or a dinner, don’t invite your friends, your brothers or sisters, your relatives, or your rich neighbors, because they might invite you back, and you would be repaid.
CSB17.13: On the contrary, when you host a banquet, <sup>ⓘ</sup>invite those who are poor, maimed, lame, or blind. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.14: And you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you; for you will be repaid <sup>ⓚ</sup>at the resurrection of the righteous.” <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.15: When <sup>ⓜ</sup> one of those who reclined at the table with him heard these things, he said to him, “Blessed is the one who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!” <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.16: Then he told him: “A man was giving a large banquet and invited many.
CSB17.17: At the time of the banquet, he sent his servant to tell those who were invited, ‘Come, because everything is now ready.’
CSB17.18: “But without exception <sup>[2]</sup> they all began to make excuses. The first one said to him, ‘I have bought a field, and I must go out and see it. I ask you to excuse me.’
CSB17.19: “Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I’m going to try them out. I ask you to excuse me.’
CSB17.20: “And another said, ‘I just got married, <sup>ⓐ</sup> and therefore I’m unable to come.’
CSB17.21: “So the servant came back and reported these things to his master. Then in anger, the master of the house told his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the city, and bring in here the poor, maimed, blind, and lame.’ <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.22: “‘Master,’ the servant said, ‘what you ordered has been done, and there’s still room.’
CSB17.23: “Then the master told the servant, ‘Go out into the highways and hedges and make them come in, so that my house may be filled.
CSB17.24: For I tell you, not one of those people who were invited will enjoy my banquet.’”
CSB17.25: Now <sup>ⓒ</sup> great crowds were traveling with him. So he turned and said to them:
CSB17.26: “If anyone comes to me <sup>ⓓ</sup>and does not hate <sup>ⓔ</sup>his own father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters — yes, and even his own life — he cannot be my disciple.
CSB17.27: Whoever does not bear his own cross <sup>ⓕ</sup> and come after me cannot be my disciple.
CSB17.28: “For which of you, wanting to build a tower, doesn’t first sit down and calculate the cost <sup>ⓖ</sup> to see if he has enough to complete it?
CSB17.29: Otherwise, after he has laid the foundation and cannot finish it, all the onlookers will begin to ridicule him,
CSB17.30: saying, ‘This man started to build and wasn’t able to finish.’
CSB17.31: “Or what king, going to war against another king, will not first sit down and decide if he is able with ten thousand to oppose the one who comes against him with twenty thousand?
CSB17.32: If not, while the other is still far off, he sends a delegation and asks for terms of peace.
CSB17.33: In the same way, therefore, every one of you who does not renounce <sup>[3]</sup>all his possessions <sup>ⓗ</sup>cannot be my disciple.
CSB17.34: “Now, <sup>ⓘ</sup> salt <sup>ⓙ</sup> is good, but if salt should lose its taste, how will it be made salty?
CSB17.35: It isn’t fit for the soil or for the manure pile; they throw it out. Let anyone who has ears to hear listen.” <sup>ⓚ</sup>

AMP.1: It happened one Sabbath, when He went for a meal at the house of one of the ruling Pharisees, that they were watching Him closely and carefully [hoping to entrap Him].
AMP.2: And there in front of Him was a man who had dropsy (extreme swelling).
AMP.3: And Jesus asked the lawyers and the Pharisees, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"
AMP.4: But they kept silent. Then He took hold of the man and healed him, and sent him on his way.
AMP.5: Then He said to them, "Which one of you, having a son or an ox that falls into a well, will not immediately pull him out on the Sabbath day?"
AMP.6: And they were unable to reply to this.
AMP.7: Now Jesus began telling a parable to the invited guests when He noticed how they had been selecting the places of honor at the table, saying to them,
AMP.8: "When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not sit down [to eat] at the place of honor, since a more distinguished person than you may have been invited by the host,
AMP.9: and he who invited both of you will come and say to you, 'Give this man your place,' and then, in disgrace you proceed to take the last place.
AMP.10: But when you are invited, go and sit down [to eat] at the last place, so that when your host comes, he will say to you, 'Friend, move up higher'; and then you will be honored in the presence of all who are at the table with you.
AMP.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled [before others], and he who habitually humbles himself (keeps a realistic self-view) will be exalted."
AMP.12: Jesus also went on to say to the one who had invited Him, "When you give a luncheon or a dinner, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or wealthy neighbors, otherwise they may also invite you in return and that will be your repayment.
AMP.13: But when you give a banquet or a reception, invite the poor, the disabled, the lame, and the blind,
AMP.14: and you will be blessed because they cannot repay you; for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous (the just, the upright)."
AMP.15: When one of those who were reclining at the table with Him heard this, he said to Him, "Blessed (happy, prosperous, to be admired) is he who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!"
AMP.16: But Jesus said to him, "A man was giving a big dinner, and he invited many guests;
AMP.17: and at the dinner hour he sent his servant to tell those who had been invited, 'Come, because everything is ready now.'
AMP.18: But they all alike began to make excuses. The first one said to him, 'I have purchased a piece of land and I have to go out and see it; please consider me excused.'
AMP.19: Another one said, 'I have purchased five yoke of oxen, and I am going to try them out; please consider me excused.'
AMP.20: And another said, 'I have [recently] married a wife, and for that reason I am unable to come.'
AMP.21: So the servant came back and reported this to his master. Then [his master,] the head of the household, became angry [at the rejections of his invitation] and said to his servant, 'Go out quickly into the streets and the lanes of the city and bring in here the poor and the disabled and the blind and the lame.'
AMP.22: And the servant [after returning] said, 'Sir, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.'
AMP.23: Then the master told the servant, 'Go out into the highways and along the hedges, and compel them to come in, so that my house may be filled [with guests].
AMP.24: For I tell you, not one of those who were invited [and declined] will taste my dinner.'"
AMP.25: Now large crowds were going along with Jesus; and He turned and said to them,
AMP.26: "If anyone comes to Me, and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, yes, and even his own life [in the sense of indifference to or relative disregard for them in comparison with his attitude toward God]—he cannot be My disciple.
AMP.27: Whoever does not carry his own cross [expressing a willingness to endure whatever may come] and follow after Me [believing in Me, conforming to My example in living and, if need be, suffering or perhaps dying because of faith in Me] cannot be My disciple.
AMP.28: For which one of you, when he wants to build a watchtower [for his guards], does not first sit down and calculate the cost, to see if he has enough to finish it?
AMP.29: Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is unable to finish [the building], all who see it will begin to ridicule him,
AMP.30: saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish!'
AMP.31: Or what king, when he sets out to meet another king in battle, will not first sit down and consider whether he is strong enough with ten thousand men to encounter the one who is coming against him with twenty thousand?
AMP.32: Or else [if he feels he is not powerful enough], while the other [king] is still a far distance away, he sends an envoy and asks for terms of peace.
AMP.33: So then, none of you can be My disciple who does not [carefully consider the cost and then for My sake] give up all his own possessions.
AMP.34: "Therefore, salt is good; but if salt has become tasteless, with what will it be seasoned?
AMP.35: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile; it is thrown away. He who has ears to hear, let him hear and heed My words."

MSG.1: One time when Jesus went for a Sabbath meal with one of the top leaders of the Pharisees, all the guests had their eyes on him, watching his every move.
MSG.2: Right before him there was a man hugely swollen in his joints.
MSG.3: So Jesus asked the religion scholars and Pharisees present, "Is it permitted to heal on the Sabbath? Yes or no?"
MSG.4: They were silent. So he took the man, healed him, and sent him on his way.
MSG.5: Then he said, "Is there anyone here who, if a child or animal fell down a well, wouldn't rush to pull him out immediately, not asking whether or not it was the Sabbath?"
MSG.6: They were stumped. There was nothing they could say to that.
MSG.7: He went on to tell a story to the guests around the table. Noticing how each had tried to elbow into the place of honor, he said,
MSG.8: "When someone invites you to dinner, don't take the place of honor. Somebody more important than you might have been invited by the host.
MSG.9: Then he'll come and call out in front of everybody, 'You're in the wrong place. The place of honor belongs to this man.' Red-faced, you'll have to make your way to the very last table, the only place left.
MSG.10: "When you're invited to dinner, go and sit at the last place. Then when the host comes he may very well say, 'Friend, come up to the front.' That will give the dinner guests something to talk about!
MSG.11: What I'm saying is, If you walk around with your nose in the air, you're going to end up flat on your face. But if you're content to be simply yourself, you will become more than yourself."
MSG.12: Then he turned to the host. "The next time you put on a dinner, don't just invite your friends and family and rich neighbors, the kind of people who will return the favor.
MSG.13: Invite some people who never get invited out, the misfits from the wrong side of the tracks.
MSG.14: You'll be—and experience—a blessing. They won't be able to return the favor, but the favor will be returned—oh, how it will be returned!—at the resurrection of God's people."
MSG.15: That triggered a response from one of the guests: "How fortunate the one who gets to eat dinner in God's kingdom!"
MSG.16: Jesus followed up. "Yes. For there was once a man who threw a great dinner party and invited many.
MSG.17: When it was time for dinner, he sent out his servant to the invited guests, saying, 'Come on in; the food's on the table.'
MSG.18: "Then they all began to beg off, one after another making excuses. The first said, 'I bought a piece of property and need to look it over. Send my regrets.'
MSG.19: "Another said, 'I just bought five teams of oxen, and I really need to check them out. Send my regrets.'
MSG.20: "And yet another said, 'I just got married and need to get home to my wife.'
MSG.21: "The servant went back and told the master what had happened. He was outraged and told the servant, 'Quickly, get out into the city streets and alleys. Collect all who look like they need a square meal, all the misfits and homeless and wretched you can lay your hands on, and bring them here.'
MSG.22: "The servant reported back, 'Master, I did what you commanded—and there's still room.'
MSG.23: "The master said, 'Then go to the country roads. Whoever you find, drag them in. I want my house full!
MSG.24: Let me tell you, not one of those originally invited is going to get so much as a bite at my dinner party.'"
MSG.25: One day when large groups of people were walking along with him, Jesus turned and told them,
MSG.26: "Anyone who comes to me but refuses to let go of father, mother, spouse, children, brothers, sisters—yes, even one's own self!—can't be my disciple.
MSG.27: Anyone who won't shoulder his own cross and follow behind me can't be my disciple.
MSG.28: "Is there anyone here who, planning to build a new house, doesn't first sit down and figure the cost so you'll know if you can complete it?
MSG.29: If you only get the foundation laid and then run out of money, you're going to look pretty foolish. Everyone passing by will poke fun at you:
MSG.31: "Or can you imagine a king going into battle against another king without first deciding whether it is possible with his ten thousand troops to face the twenty thousand troops of the other?
MSG.32: And if he decides he can't, won't he send an emissary and work out a truce?
MSG.33: "Simply put, if you're not willing to take what is dearest to you, whether plans or people, and kiss it good-bye, you can't be my disciple.
MSG.34: "Salt is excellent. But if the salt goes flat, it's useless, good for nothing. "Are you listening to this? Really listening?"

NIV.1:
Jesus at a Pharisee’s House
One Sabbath, when Jesus went to eat in the house of a prominent Pharisee, he was being carefully watched.
NIV.2: There in front of him was a man suffering from dropsy.
NIV.3: Jesus asked the Pharisees and experts in the law, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not?”
NIV.4: But they remained silent. So taking hold of the man, he healed him and sent him away.
NIV.5: Then he asked them, “If one of you has a son or an ox that falls into a well on the Sabbath day, will you not immediately pull him out?”
NIV.6: And they had nothing to say.
NIV.7: When he noticed how the guests picked the places of honor at the table, he told them this parable:
NIV.8: “When someone invites you to a wedding feast, do not take the place of honor, for a person more distinguished than you may have been invited.
NIV.9: If so, the host who invited both of you will come and say to you, ‘Give this man your seat.’ Then, humiliated, you will have to take the least important place.
NIV.10: But when you are invited, take the lowest place, so that when your host comes, he will say to you, ‘Friend, move up to a better place.’ Then you will be honored in the presence of all your fellow guests.
NIV.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.”
NIV.12: Then Jesus said to his host, “When you give a luncheon or dinner, do not invite your friends, your brothers or relatives, or your rich neighbors; if you do, they may invite you back and so you will be repaid.
NIV.13: But when you give a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind,
NIV.14: and you will be blessed. Although they cannot repay you, you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.”
NIV.15:
The Parable of the Great Banquet
When one of those at the table with him heard this, he said to Jesus, “Blessed is the man who will eat at the feast in the kingdom of God.”
NIV.16: Jesus replied: “A certain man was preparing a great banquet and invited many guests.
NIV.17: At the time of the banquet he sent his servant to tell those who had been invited, ‘Come, for everything is now ready.’
NIV.18: “But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said, ‘I have just bought a field, and I must go and see it. Please excuse me.’
NIV.19: “Another said, ‘I have just bought five yoke of oxen, and I’m on my way to try them out. Please excuse me.’
NIV.20: “Still another said, ‘I just got married, so I can’t come.’
NIV.21: “The servant came back and reported this to his master. Then the owner of the house became angry and ordered his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the town and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind and the lame.’
NIV.22: “‘Sir,’ the servant said, ‘what you ordered has been done, but there is still room.’
NIV.23: “Then the master told his servant, ‘Go out to the roads and country lanes and make them come in, so that my house will be full.
NIV.24: I tell you, not one of those men who were invited will get a taste of my banquet.’”
NIV.25:
The Cost of Being a Disciple
Large crowds were traveling with Jesus, and turning to them he said:
NIV.26: “If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, his wife and children, his brothers and sisters—yes, even his own life—he cannot be my disciple.
NIV.27: And anyone who does not carry his cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
NIV.28: “Suppose one of you wants to build a tower. Will he not first sit down and estimate the cost to see if he has enough money to complete it?
NIV.29: For if he lays the foundation and is not able to finish it, everyone who sees it will ridicule him,
NIV.30: saying, ‘This fellow began to build and was not able to finish.’
NIV.31: “Or suppose a king is about to go to war against another king. Will he not first sit down and consider whether he is able with ten thousand men to oppose the one coming against him with twenty thousand?
NIV.32: If he is not able, he will send a delegation while the other is still a long way off and will ask for terms of peace.
NIV.33: In the same way, any of you who does not give up everything he has cannot be my disciple.
NIV.34: “Salt is good, but if it loses its saltiness, how can it be made salty again?
NIV.35:
It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile; it is thrown out.
“He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”

NRSVCE.1: On one occasion when Jesus was going to the house of a leader of the Pharisees to eat a meal on the sabbath, they were watching him closely.
NRSVCE.2: Just then, in front of him, there was a man who had dropsy.
NRSVCE.3: And Jesus asked the lawyers and Pharisees, “Is it lawful to cure people on the sabbath, or not?”
NRSVCE.4: But they were silent. So Jesus took him and healed him, and sent him away.
NRSVCE.5: Then he said to them, “If one of you has a child or an ox that has fallen into a well, will you not immediately pull it out on a sabbath day?”
NRSVCE.6: And they could not reply to this.
NRSVCE.7: When he noticed how the guests chose the places of honor, he told them a parable.
NRSVCE.8: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding banquet, do not sit down at the place of honor, in case someone more distinguished than you has been invited by your host;
NRSVCE.9: and the host who invited both of you may come and say to you, ‘Give this person your place,’ and then in disgrace you would start to take the lowest place.
NRSVCE.10: But when you are invited, go and sit down at the lowest place, so that when your host comes, he may say to you, ‘Friend, move up higher’; then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at the table with you.
NRSVCE.11: For all who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted.”
NRSVCE.12: He said also to the one who had invited him, “When you give a luncheon or a dinner, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors, in case they may invite you in return, and you would be repaid.
NRSVCE.13: But when you give a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind.
NRSVCE.14: And you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you, for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.”
NRSVCE.15: One of the dinner guests, on hearing this, said to him, “Blessed is anyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!”
NRSVCE.16: Then Jesus said to him, “Someone gave a great dinner and invited many.
NRSVCE.17: At the time for the dinner he sent his slave to say to those who had been invited, ‘Come; for everything is ready now.’
NRSVCE.18: But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said to him, ‘I have bought a piece of land, and I must go out and see it; please accept my regrets.’
NRSVCE.19: Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to try them out; please accept my regrets.’
NRSVCE.20: Another said, ‘I have just been married, and therefore I cannot come.’
NRSVCE.21: So the slave returned and reported this to his master. Then the owner of the house became angry and said to his slave, ‘Go out at once into the streets and lanes of the town and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.’
NRSVCE.22: And the slave said, ‘Sir, what you ordered has been done, and there is still room.’
NRSVCE.23: Then the master said to the slave, ‘Go out into the roads and lanes, and compel people to come in, so that my house may be filled.
NRSVCE.24: For I tell you, none of those who were invited will taste my dinner.’”
NRSVCE.25: Now large crowds were traveling with him; and he turned and said to them,
NRSVCE.26: “Whoever comes to me and does not hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and even life itself, cannot be my disciple.
NRSVCE.27: Whoever does not carry the cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
NRSVCE.28: For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not first sit down and estimate the cost, to see whether he has enough to complete it?
NRSVCE.29: Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it will begin to ridicule him,
NRSVCE.30: saying, ‘This fellow began to build and was not able to finish.’
NRSVCE.31: Or what king, going out to wage war against another king, will not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to oppose the one who comes against him with twenty thousand?
NRSVCE.32: If he cannot, then, while the other is still far away, he sends a delegation and asks for the terms of peace.
NRSVCE.33: So therefore, none of you can become my disciple if you do not give up all your possessions.
NRSVCE.34: “Salt is good; but if salt has lost its taste, how can its saltiness be restored?
NRSVCE.35: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile; they throw it away. Let anyone with ears to hear listen!”

NET.1: Now one Sabbath when Jesus went to dine at the house of a leader of the Pharisees, they were watching him closely.
NET.2: There right in front of him was a man suffering from dropsy.
NET.3: So Jesus asked the experts in religious law and the Pharisees, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not?”
NET.4: But they remained silent. So Jesus took hold of the man, healed him, and sent him away.
NET.5: Then he said to them, “Which of you, if you have a son or an ox that has fallen into a well on a Sabbath day, will not immediately pull him out?”
NET.6: But they could not reply to this.
NET.7: Then when Jesus noticed how the guests chose the places of honor, he told them a parable. He said to them,
NET.8: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not take the place of honor, because a person more distinguished than you may have been invited by your host.
NET.9: So the host who invited both of you will come and say to you, ‘Give this man your place.’ Then, ashamed, you will begin to move to the least important place.
NET.10: But when you are invited, go and take the least important place, so that when your host approaches he will say to you, ‘Friend, move up here to a better place.’ Then you will be honored in the presence of all who share the meal with you.
NET.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.”
NET.12: He said also to the man who had invited him, “When you host a dinner or a banquet, don’t invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors so you can be invited by them in return and get repaid.
NET.13: But when you host an elaborate meal, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind.
NET.14: Then you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you, for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.”
NET.15: When one of those at the meal with Jesus heard this, he said to him, “Blessed is everyone who will feast in the kingdom of God!”
NET.16: But Jesus said to him, “A man once gave a great banquet and invited many guests.
NET.17: At the time for the banquet he sent his slave to tell those who had been invited, ‘Come, because everything is now ready.’
NET.18: But one after another they all began to make excuses. The first said to him, ‘I have bought a field, and I must go out and see it. Please excuse me.’
NET.19: Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going out to examine them. Please excuse me.’
NET.20: Another said, ‘I just got married, and I cannot come.’
NET.21: So the slave came back and reported this to his master. Then the master of the household was furious and said to his slave, ‘Go out quickly to the streets and alleys of the city, and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.’
NET.22: Then the slave said, ‘Sir, what you instructed has been done, and there is still room.’
NET.23: So the master said to his slave, ‘Go out to the highways and country roads and urge people to come in, so that my house will be filled.
NET.24: For I tell you, not one of those individuals who were invited will taste my banquet!’”
NET.25: Now large crowds were accompanying Jesus, and turning to them he said,
NET.26: “If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother, and wife and children, and brothers and sisters, and even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.
NET.27: Whoever does not carry his own cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
NET.28: For which of you, wanting to build a tower, doesn’t sit down first and compute the cost to see if he has enough money to complete it?
NET.29: Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish the tower, all who see it will begin to make fun of him.
NET.30: They will say, ‘This man began to build and was not able to finish!’
NET.31: Or what king, going out to confront another king in battle, will not sit down first and determine whether he is able with ten thousand to oppose the one coming against him with twenty thousand?
NET.32: If he cannot succeed, he will send a representative while the other is still a long way off and ask for terms of peace.
NET.33: In the same way therefore not one of you can be my disciple if he does not renounce all his own possessions.
NET.34: “Salt is good, but if salt loses its flavor, how can its flavor be restored?
NET.35: It is of no value for the soil or for the manure pile; it is to be thrown out. The one who has ears to hear had better listen!”
